description: ADIs must implement a credit risk management framework appropriate to their size, business mix and complexity, including a credit risk appetite statement, management strategy, policies, a designated credit risk function, management information systems, and an independent review process.

ADIs must maintain a well-documented credit risk appetite statement articulating credit risk appetite, credit risk tolerance, processes for setting and monitoring tolerances, and the timing and process for review.

ADIs must maintain a well-documented credit risk management strategy reflecting their credit risk appetite, credit risk profile, and market and macroeconomic conditions, including willingness to accept credit risk by exposure type, sector, geography, currency and maturity.
The Board must review and approve the ADI's credit risk appetite and credit risk management strategy on at least an annual basis.

Senior management must implement the Board-approved credit risk management strategy, develop and implement credit risk policies and processes, and ensure regular independent internal reviews of credit origination, assessment, approval and management functions.

ADIs must adopt and implement prudent, well-documented policies and processes covering the full credit life-cycle, including origination, assessment, approval, and ongoing monitoring, addressing target markets, portfolio mix, limits, approval authorities, overrides, and early identification of problem exposures.

ADIs must develop and implement policies and processes to ensure adequate portfolio diversification, including setting targets for portfolio mix and prudent limits on exposures to higher risk borrowers, products, industry sectors and geographical locations.

ADIs must recognise and address risks from different credit origination channels, applying higher diligence where decisions are made distant from the borrower or collateral, and must have sound oversight processes and documentation where third parties are involved in origination.

ADIs must establish sound credit assessment and approval criteria, undertaking comprehensive assessment of borrower credit risk primarily based on repayment capacity, and must not place undue reliance on collateral or external credit ratings.

For exposures to individuals, credit assessment must include consideration of the purpose and structure of the exposure, the borrower's risk profile, repayment history and capacity under various scenarios, expenses, proposed terms and conditions, and adequacy of collateral and guarantees.

ADIs must have prudent credit risk policies covering the acceptability of collateral, appropriate valuation processes (including prior to drawdown and on an ongoing basis), and processes to ensure collateral remains enforceable and realisable, with all valuations appraised independently from the credit origination and approval process.

Collateral valuations must reflect fair values taking into account prevailing market conditions, including time taken for liquidation or realisation, and must account for the likelihood of external events such as fire, drought and flood where relevant.
ADIs must establish appropriate LVR limits to minimise the risk that property collateral will be insufficient to cover repayment shortfalls, and must ensure appropriate scrutiny of high LVR lending.

ADIs must have a clearly established and documented credit approval process identifying approval authorities for each exposure, and must ensure personnel have appropriate experience and knowledge to exercise prudent credit judgement.

ADIs must have prudent policies and processes for overrides, waivers or exceptions, including clear identification of approval authorities and limits on allowable overrides, with regular reporting of such exposures to internal governance bodies.

Where a third party undertakes credit assessment or approval, ADIs must implement appropriate oversight processes and regularly monitor and test the integrity of the third-party assessment to ensure alignment with the ADI's criteria.

ADIs must maintain appropriate systems for ongoing credit portfolio administration, monitoring individual exposures and overall portfolio quality, and measuring credit risk across all on- and off-balance sheet activities, including management information systems that identify risk concentrations.

Where appropriate for the scope, scale and complexity of operations, ADIs must have an internal credit risk grading system with prudent policies governing its development, validation, operation and oversight, covering as much of the credit portfolio as possible.

ADIs must undertake regular portfolio-level and risk-specific stress testing of credit exposures under severe but plausible adverse conditions, with results considered in limit setting and communicated to the Board and senior management, and stress testing models must be independently validated.

ADIs must establish a system of independent, regular reviews of credit risk management processes with results communicated directly to the Board and senior management, and must enforce internal controls ensuring overrides are reported in a timely manner.

ADIs must have appropriate systems for early remedial action on problem exposures, an effective workout program with segregation from the originating function, and prudent policies to recover as much of an exposure as reasonably achievable.

ADIs must classify credit risk exposures as performing or non-performing, with classification also required at 30, 60 and 90 days past-due, supported by adequate documentation, and must regularly review exposures to ensure appropriate classification, provisioning and write-offs.

The Board must receive timely and appropriate information on the credit portfolio condition, including classification of exposures as performing and non-performing, provisioning levels, credit risk review results, and trends in asset quality and expected credit losses.
Valuation, classification and provisioning for material non-performing exposures must be conducted on an individual exposure basis.

Non-performing exposures must be measured as the whole exposure amount; collateral must not influence categorisation or past-due status; and off-balance sheet exposures, commitments and derivatives must be classified as non-performing where timely full repayment is unlikely.

An ADI may reclassify a non-performing exposure as performing only when the borrower has no exposure 90 days or more past-due, repayments have been made over a continuous period of at least 90 days (or six months for restructured exposures), the borrower's situation has improved, and the exposure no longer meets the non-performing definition.

An exposure is classified as non-performing (in default) when the borrower is 90 days or more past-due on a credit obligation, with specific conditions for when an exposure subject to a regular repayment schedule is considered 90 days past-due.

Restructured exposures must remain classified as restructured until exit criteria are met, including a six-month probation period of timely repayments under revised terms and resolution of the borrower's financial difficulty; new forbearance during the probation period restarts the probation period.

ADIs must adopt sound methodologies for assessing and measuring credit losses on all exposures consistent with Australian Accounting Standards, with aggregate provisions adequate and models appropriately validated, applying experienced credit judgement including forward-looking macroeconomic information.

Prescribed provisions are minimum provisions that an ADI must hold; any excess provisions established under Australian Accounting Standards must continue to be reported as specific or general provisions for regulatory capital and APRA reporting purposes.
ADIs may outsource aspects of credit risk management including origination and assessment, but must comply with CPS 231 for all outsourced functions.

Valuation of security must be undertaken prior to drawdown and on an ongoing basis where appropriate, and must reflect fair values taking into account market conditions, liquidity, disposal costs and the likelihood of external events affecting asset values.
ADIs using internal appraisers for security valuations must have policies addressing conflicts of interest and the qualifications and experience required of internal appraisers.

ADIs relying on external valuers must establish robust selection and review mechanisms considering professional qualifications, relevant experience, breadth of expertise and appropriate professional insurance cover.

ADIs using alternative valuation methods such as desktop, kerbside or automated valuation methods must have appropriate policies addressing when such methods are used, monitoring and validation of valuation data, and adequate haircuts for conservatism.
Where an ADI relies on a panel of approved valuation professionals, the panel must be regularly reviewed at least annually.

ADIs must require valuers and appraisers to adopt the valuation standards and practices of relevant professional bodies, and must have policies ensuring reliability of valuation processes through independent and regular internal reviews and formal reviews by an independent valuer.

For property security valuations, ADIs must assume a marketing period of up to 12 months (up to 24 months for specialised or unusual properties), with market conditions and asset values assumed static over the marketing period.

Property assets must be based on fair values using conservative estimates for future income streams; valuations based on highest and best use rather than existing use must be adequately supported and documented.

Prescribed provisions must be treated as specific provisions for capital and APRA reporting purposes, reported on an after-tax basis, with any associated deferred tax asset removed, and deducted from Common Equity Tier 1 Capital.
Category One exposures (well-secured residential mortgage exposures with LVR no more than 80% or 100% LMI insured) require no prescribed provisions.

Category Two exposures (residential first mortgage with LVR net of LMI greater than 80% but no more than 100%, 90 days or more past-due) require minimum prescribed provisions ranging from 0% to 20% of the outstanding balance depending on the term of payments past-due.

Category Three exposures (all exposures not in Categories One, Two or Four, including personal and commercial loans and residential mortgages with LVR net of LMI greater than 100%) require minimum prescribed provisions ranging from 0% to 100% of the outstanding balance depending on the term of payments past-due.

Category Four exposures (overdrawn savings accounts and revolving exposures such as credit cards, overdrafts and lines of credit) require minimum prescribed provisions ranging from 0% to 100% of the outstanding balance depending on the period of irregularity.
